Name: SS Yongala Wreck
Description: One of the worlds best wreck dives, the SS Yongala sank in 1911 and is now an artificial reef teeming with marine life. The 109-meter vessel attracts massive schools of fish and pelagic species.
Marine_Life: Bull sharks, eagle rays, sea snakes, giant grouper, trevally, barracuda
Hazards: Strong currents, penetration hazards, marine life encounters
Facilities: Day boat access, advanced certification required
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal, as it's an isolated offshore wreck.
Viz_Triggers: Wet/cyclone season (January-April) can reduce visibility, large schools of bait fish can be present
Viz_Seasonal: Generally best during the dry season from May to November (15-30m). Wet/cyclone season (January-April) visibility approx 10-15m

Name: Muiron Island
Description: Beautiful coral gardens and diverse marine life around Muiron Islands, featuring excellent visibility and abundant fish species. Perfect for intermediate divers looking to explore pristine reef systems.
Marine_Life: Manta rays, whale sharks, tropical fish, coral trout, reef sharks
Facilities: Boat access only, no shore facilities
Viz_Triggers: Weather conditions, plankton in water
Viz_Seasonal: Variable, depending on plankton season

Name: Hat Head Bommie
Description: Isolated reef outcrop with ledges and pelagics.

Name: Black Rock – Forster
Description: Rocky outcrop with strong currents and deep ledges.

Name: HMAS Brisbane
Description: Well-prepared wreck with easy access, great for all diver levels.
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal coastal runoff from nearby national park.
Viz_Triggers: Strong currents, large swells impacting the wreck, and heavy rainfall.
Viz_Seasonal: Generally excellent visibility (15-30m) during calm conditions. Can be reduced after heavy rains or large swells.

Name: Arno Bay Jetty
Description: Sheltered jetty good for training and macro life.

Name: Aldinga Drop-off
Description: Sheer underwater cliff with ledges, overhangs, and caves.

Name: American River Wharf
Description: Easy estuarine dive with macro life and grassy bottom.

Name: Ningaloo Reef
Description: Western Australias fringing coral reef offering easy shore access and pristine coral gardens. Famous for whale shark encounters and manta ray cleaning stations.
Marine_Life: Whale sharks, manta rays, reef sharks, turtles, coral trout
Hazards: Strong shore surge, seasonal marine life
Compass_Bearing: Variable
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal coastal runoff from arid coastline.
Viz_Triggers: Weather conditions, daily changes
Viz_Seasonal: Best from March to October

Name: Exmouth Navy Jetty
Description: Concrete jetty home to juvenile fish, octopus and nudibranchs.
Viz_Triggers: Tides, weather, plankton

Name: Fish Rock Cave
Description: Ocean cave and shark hotspot with tunnel dives and big marine life.
Viz_Seasonal: Always clear inside the cave

Name: Montague Island
Description: Diving at Montague Island offers an unforgettable experience with crystal-clear waters, playful Australian fur seals, and vibrant marine life.
Marine_Life:
Fur seals 
Grey nurse sharks 
Reef fish including angelfish, butterflyfish, parrotfish, kingfish, and trevally
Wobbegong and Port Jackson sharks resting on the seafloor

Sea turtles 
Dolphins
Humpback whales
Little penguins 
Stingrays and eagle rays
Hazards:
Strong currents – Especially on the eastern and southern sides; can challenge inexperienced divers

Cold water temperatures – Thermoclines and upwellings can cause sudden drops in temperature

Surge and swell – Common around rocky entry/exit points; can affect visibility and diver control

Limited visibility – Variable, especially after storms or heavy rain

Marine life – Although mostly safe, close interaction with seals or large sharks requires caution

Boat traffic – Popular for tours and fishing; surface marker buoys are essential

Rough surface conditions – Wind and wave exposure can make boat entry and exit difficult
Depth – Some dive sites exceed 18 metres;
Facilities:
Guided dive tours
Gear rental available
Air fills onboard
Purpose-built dive boats
Accommodation packages nearby
Viz_Seasonal: Year-round

Name: Julian Rocks – Cod Hole
Description: Swim-through with large cod and pelagics.
Viz_Triggers: Can experience some current on the northern side
Viz_Seasonal: Almost always accessible. Winter often sees huge schools of fish

Name: Oak Park
Description: Rocky reef with gullies and swim-throughs, popular for macro photography.
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal - Gunnamatta Bay is a sheltered estuary; some stormwater from Cronulla residential areas
Viz_Triggers: NE swell reduces viz; clearest on calm SW wind days; post-rain turbidity from bay drains lasts 1-2 days
Viz_Seasonal: Summer: 8-15m, Winter: 5-10m; best on calm mornings after settled weather; autumn most reliable

Name: Blue Holes
Description: Unique coastal reef with natural "holes" and excellent snorkeling/diving.
Viz_Triggers: Avoid strong swell or persistent onshore winds which can reduce clarity
Viz_Seasonal: Usually excellent in summer when winds ease

Name: Orford Reef Giant Kelp Forest
Description: Rare giant kelp dive with deep walls and cold-water marine life.

Name: Black Point Jetty
Description: Shallow jetty with sponge growth and juvenile fish, good for macro.
Visibility: Improved visibility on the western side of the peninsula
Viz_Triggers: Strong easterly winds can improve visibility

Name: Chowder Bay
Description: Sheltered harbour site with good muck diving and macro subjects.
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal - protected harbour location at Mosman; small local stormwater catchment
Viz_Triggers: Very protected; general harbour turbidity after heavy rain; boat mooring area creates minor sediment disturbance
Viz_Seasonal: Consistent 5-8m year-round; rarely drops below 4m; summer slightly clearer than winter

Name: Poor Knights Islands
Description: New Zealands premier diving destination featuring underwater archways, caves, and dramatic wall diving. Subtropical marine life thrives in this marine reserve.
Marine_Life: Blue mao mao, moray eels, stingrays, kingfish, nudibranchs
Hazards: Strong currents, cave environments, depth changes
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal, as it's an isolated offshore island group.
Viz_Triggers: Influenced by the East Australian Current, nutrient-rich upwellings
Viz_Seasonal: Summer months offer warmer water and better visibility. Spring can have green-tinted, nutrient-rich waters

Name: Darwin Harbour Wrecks
Description: WWII wrecks with low visibility but fascinating historical appeal and reef life.

Name: Lord Howe Island
Description: World Heritage reef and sea stack with sharks, schools, and rare fish.
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal coastal runoff from natural island and protected lagoon.
Viz_Triggers: Strong easterly swells, heavy rainfall impacting lagoon outflow, and seasonal plankton blooms.
Viz_Seasonal: Excellent visibility from October to May (Australian spring and summer). Average of 20-30 meters visibility during dive season (September-May)

Name: Terrigal Haven
Description: Terrigal Haven is the Central Coast’s quintessential training and relaxation dive site. It may lack the dramatic topography of deep offshore reefs, but it compensates with calm waters, gentle reefs, and marine diversity that never fails to surprise. Whether it’s your first descent or your fiftieth return, the Haven remains a place where every dive feels personal — a sheltered corner of the sea that invites you to slow down and simply explore.
Marine_Life:
Old wives – easily recognised by their silver stripes and spiny dorsal fins

Wrasse and leatherjackets – in vivid greens and yellows around the kelp beds

Moray eels – hiding in rock crevices, especially near the headland wall

Octopus and cuttlefish – masters of camouflage among the sand and sponge gardens

Schooling fish – yellowtail, mado, and sweep forming shimmering mid-water clouds

Nudibranchs – abundant in cooler months, especially on the sponges near the reef edge

Port Jackson sharks – resting on sand patches or under ledges during the day
Facilities:
Public toilets and showers: Well maintained and located beside the car park, including outdoor cold showers for rinsing.

Rinse station: Near the boat ramp — ideal for cleaning masks, fins, and cameras.

Picnic area: Grassed foreshore with shaded tables, perfect for debriefs or lunch breaks.

Cafés and food: Several cafés and restaurants overlook the Haven, including The Cove and The Reef Restaurant. A short walk uphill leads to Terrigal’s main street with bakeries and dive-friendly coffee spots.

Dive shop: Terrigal Dive Centre
 is located on the Haven itself, offering air and nitrox fills, hire gear, courses, guided dives, and boat charters to Skillion Reef and the ex-HMAS Adelaide wreck.

Accommodation: Terrigal offers hotels, serviced apartments, and holiday rentals within a few hundred metres of the Haven.
Viz_Triggers: Prevailing sea conditions, adverse easterly weather

Name: Skillion Caves
Description: Scillion Caves sits below the dramatic sandstone headland known as The Skillion at Terrigal. This site features swim-throughs, small caverns, and sponge-covered rock faces. It’s a rewarding dive for experienced divers due to depth, surge, and navigation challenges. Access is usually via boat from Terrigal Haven or as an extended shore dive for advanced divers.
Marine_Life: Home to wobbegongs, blue gropers, and schooling sweep. Nudibranchs and sponges coat the cave entrances. Divers often spot Port Jackson sharks resting in the sand gutters, moray eels, and the occasional kingfish passing by. In winter, giant cuttlefish display along the reef.
Hazards: Strong surge near the cave mouths in swell. Potential entanglement if entering tight sections. Watch for boat traffic above. Only suitable in calm seas.
Facilities: Access via Terrigal Haven with parking, toilets, showers, and air fills at Terrigal Dive Centre. Café and restaurant options on the foreshore.
Viz_Triggers: Calm sea conditions required for diving

Name: Freshwater Reef
Description: Offshore reef with soft corals and great fish life.
Viz_Triggers: Protected location

Name: Weebubbie Cave
Description: Gin-clear water and massive white limestone chambers.
Visibility: Dependent on artificial light (dark cave system)

Name: Terrigal HMAS Adelaide (Currently Closed)
Description: The HMAS Adelaide is a purpose-sunk ex-Royal Australian Navy guided missile frigate resting upright on the sandy seabed just off Terrigal. Scuttled in 2011, the 138-metre vessel has transformed into one of Australia’s most iconic artificial reefs. Her decks, passageways and control rooms now brim with marine life, offering divers an extraordinary blend of naval history and ocean biodiversity. The wreck sits in clear, temperate water and is accessible to qualified divers under permit.
Marine_Life: The wreck supports dense growths of soft coral, sponges and kelp. Large schools of yellowtail, kingfish and sweep patrol the superstructure. Look for wobbegong sharks resting on the deck, eastern blue gropers cruising the hull, and the occasional grey nurse shark gliding through the shadows. Nudibranchs and pygmy leatherjackets inhabit the smaller recesses, adding bursts of colour to the steel corridors.
Hazards: Penetration diving presents entanglement and disorientation risks, entry is restricted to trained wreck divers with reels and torches. Depth and current can vary, and a safety stop is required on ascent. Fishing lines and loose cables occasionally hang from the structure.
Facilities: Terrigal Haven offers a boat ramp, parking, dive shop air fills, toilets and showers. Charter boats run regular trips from Terrigal Haven and The Haven Boat Ramp. Permits and tokens are available through local dive centres.

Name: North Bondi Bommie
Description: Rocky bommie with good fish life and occasional pelagics.
Nearby_Runoff: Same Bondi Beach stormwater as North Bondi Rocks - large urban catchment from Bondi and surrounds
Viz_Triggers: Same as North Bondi Rocks - northerly swells and rain events reduce viz; bommie surge from any easterly swell
Viz_Seasonal: Same as North Bondi Rocks: Summer 8-12m, Autumn best (10-15m), Winter 6-10m, Spring 5-8m
corroboration_sources: PADI website (https://www.padi.com/dive-site/australia/north-bondi/) - describes it as an underrated dive site with abundant marine life including sharks, rays, and "Lizzo" the blue groper. Also mentions "The Cathedral" swimthrough.

Name: The Colours
Description: Vibrant reef with sponges and soft corals, popular photography site.

Name: Long Reef Wall
Description: Steep drop-off with pelagics and occasional sharks.
corroboration_sources: ProDive website (https://www.prodive.com.au/Article/21/about/Sydney+Dive+Site+of+the+Week+March+25+2014+Long+Reef/by/Marcelle+de+Groot/under/Dive+Sites) - confirms rock wall, depth, and "The Cathedral" swim-through.

Name: Henderson's Rock
Description: Renowned for its dramatic topography, featuring deep ledges, kelp-covered gutters, and substantial caverns. It is a critical habitat for the endangered Grey Nurse Shark, making it a highly sought-after location for experienced divers
Region: Moreton Island, South East Queensland, Australia
Marine_Life: Grey Nurse Sharks (seasonal), Wobbegong sharks, Ornate Crayfish, Brown-banded Catsharks, Bull Rays, and diverse reef fish.
Hazards: Strong currents, surge, overhead environments (caves/caverns), and entanglement risks (thick kelp).
Facilities: None (Offshore site; accessible via charter boat from Brisbane or Gold Coast).
corroboration_sources: Visit Brisbane (https://visit.brisbane.qld.au/things-to-do/moreton-bay-region/natural-attractions/henderson-rock-dive-site-8495) and Queensland.com (https://www.queensland.com/us/en/things-to-do/attractions/p-56b26596aeeeaaf773cf8495-henderson-rock-dive-site) - both confirm deep ledges, overhangs, crayfish, and seasonal Grey Nurse Shark schooling.

Name: Smith Rock
Description: Smith Rock is one of the most significant and notorious dive sites in South East Queensland. Known as a "ship-killer," this jagged granite outcrop has claimed several vessels, including the Aarhus, Marietta Dal, and St. Paul. It is a site characterized by dramatic underwater topography, including deep gutters, caves, and swim-throughs, and is often frequented by large pelagic species.
Best_Time: Winter (May–August) for calmer seas and better visibility; Slack water is essential.
Marine_Life: Marine Life: Home to a high concentration of painted crayfish, wobbegong sharks, and turtles. You can also spot brown-banded bamboo sharks, moray eels, and schools of yellowtail kingfish. During winter, migrating Humpback whales are frequently heard and occasionally seen nearby.
Hazards:
Strong Currents: The site is highly exposed to the East Australian Current.
Surge: Shallow sections can experience heavy surge, making it dangerous near the rock face.
Navigation: The complex cave system requires excellent buoyancy and navigation skills.
Facilities: No facilities on-site. The site is only accessible by boat via commercial operators from Brisbane or the Gold Coast.
corroboration_sources: Queensland.com (https://www.queensland.com/au/en/things-to-do/attractions/p-599122a391ebab18474a7972-smith-rock-dive-site) - confirms jagged rocky outcrop, shipwrecks, and location off Moreton Island.

Name: Kingscote Jetty
Description: Easy jetty dive on Kangaroo Island with sandy bottom and jetty pylons.
Viz_Triggers: Harmful algal blooms can impact visibility

Name: Port Hughes Barge Wreck
Description: Sunken barge with good visibility, fish life and growth.
Visibility: Variable (typically good, but can be affected by tidal currents and sediment)
Viz_Triggers: Tidal currents, sediment disturbance from jetty activity

Name: Voodoo
Description: Located on the southern headland of Botany Bay in New South Wales, Voodoo is a premier shore dive known for its dramatic underwater topography and vibrant marine life. It is often considered one of the best dives in the Sydney region for those with the experience to handle the entry.
Marine_Life: Weedy Sea Dragons, Giant Cuttlefish, Port Jackson sharks, Wobbegongs, and large schools of Yellowtail. Nudibranchs
Hazards: The entry and exit require a "giant stride" off a rock ledge and a timed exit onto a slippery rock platform. Heavy surge can make the gutters dangerous, and the site is highly exposed to southerly and easterly swell
Facilities: There is a car park nearby within the National Park, but no dedicated dive facilities or freshwater showers at the immediate site.
Nearby_Runoff:
Potter Point Sewage Outfall: Located approximately 400m-600m West/North-West; releases treated effluent that can drift over the site during NE winds or southerly currents.
Boat Harbour Stormwater: Direct runoff from the industrial basin and historical sandmining ponds to the West; can introduce heavy silt and tannins after significant rainfall.
Port Hacking Estuarine Outflow: Located to the South-West; heavy rain in the Sutherland Shire sends sediment plumes north that wrap around the point.
Viz_Triggers:
Negative (Rain): Visibility often drops below 5m (or as low as 2m) following heavy rain (>10mm) due to sediment from Boat Harbour.
Negative (Winds): Moderate to strong winds from any direction except the South/West can "blow out" the site. North-Easterly winds specifically push the Potter Point plume toward the reef.
Negative (Swell): Site is highly exposed; avoid if any swell other than SW to NW is greater than 0.5m.
Positive (Tide): Incoming/High tide is ideal, pushing clean ocean water from the South/East onto the reef.
Positive (Current): A North-to-South current is favourable as it pulls outfall effluent away from the dive zone.
Viz_Seasonal:
Autumn (Best Baseline): Typically offers the most stable conditions and is often cited as the best time for waves and steady visibility.
Winter (Clarity Peak): June to August provides the best visibility, frequently exceeding 15m–20m due to consistent southerly swells bringing in clean oceanic water.
Spring (Worst/Variable): October and November are historically the worst for visibility (averaging poor on ~28% of dives) due to increased wind and potential early plankton blooms.

Summer (Upwelling): Generally good (average 10m–12m), but susceptible to "greener" water from localized upwelling events.
corroboration_sources: Abyss Scuba Diving (https://www.abyss.com.au/voodoo) - confirms Voodoo as a top shore dive in Sydney with stunning rock formations and diverse marine life.

Name: Whyalla Jetty
Description: Home of the giant cuttlefish aggregation during winter months.
Viz_Triggers: Tides and wind can significantly impact visibility

Name: Shelly Beach
Description: Popular Sydney shore dive with calm conditions and abundant temperate marine life.
Marine_Life: Dusky whaler sharks, cuttlefish, wobbegongs, blue gropers, nudibranchs
Nearby_Runoff: Manly Lagoon stormwater drain approximately 200m north; significant catchment from Manly and surrounds
Viz_Triggers: Drops sharply after rain from Manly Lagoon outflow within 2-4 hours; southerly swell over 1.5m reduces viz to 2-4m; strong NE chop stirs sand in shallows
Viz_Seasonal: Summer avg: 10m, Winter avg: 8m; Spring worst (plankton blooms 5m); Autumn most stable (8-12m)
corroboration_sources: Diveplanit.com (https://www.diveplanit.com/dive-site/shelly-beach/) and Sydney Dive Charters (https://www.sydneydivecharters.com.au/dives/guided-manly-shore-dive/) - confirm Shelly Beach as a popular shore dive in Cabbage Tree Bay Aquatic Reserve.

Name: Cape Banks Caverns
Description: Caverns and overhangs with surge channels and deep ledges.
Nearby_Runoff: Cape Banks headland - minimal direct runoff; exposed headland with little development
Viz_Triggers: Exposed at southern Botany Bay entrance; SE swell reduces viz significantly; sand channels inside caverns cloud quickly with surge
Viz_Seasonal: Summer: 8-15m (best on calm NE mornings); Winter: 6-10m; worst during sustained SE swell

Name: Bare Island
Description: One of Sydney's most popular shore dives with great macro life and calm conditions.
Marine_Life: Seahorses, octopus, weedy sea dragons, nudibranchs
Nearby_Runoff: Minor stormwater from La Perouse suburb; some Botany Bay industrial runoff during heavy rain
Viz_Triggers: Strong SE wind and swell reduces viz to 3-5m; north winds bring clearest water; post-rain turbidity lasts 1-2 days
Viz_Seasonal: Summer: 8-12m, Winter: 6-10m; best on north to northeast winds; worst during sustained SE weather
corroboration_sources: PADI website (https://www.padi.com/dive-site/australia/bare-island-2/) - confirms Bare Island as a popular shore dive in Sydney with diverse marine life, including weedy sea dragons, seahorses, octopus, and nudibranchs.

Name: Wedge Island Caves
Description: Cave system with dramatic walls and pelagics.
Visibility: Generally good, especially in the bay behind the island where conditions are often calm
Viz_Triggers: Water conditions in the bay are often calm, aiding visibility

Name: Hardwicke Bay Jetty
Description: Quiet jetty dive with sponge growth and sheltered conditions.
corroboration_sources: Snorkel Spots (https://snorkelspots.com/snork-the-yorke/) and Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hardwicke_Bay) - confirm Hardwicke Bay as a snorkel spot with a jetty. General Yorke Peninsula jetties are known for macro life, supporting the description.

Name: Corny Point Lagoon
Description: Lagoon dive with juvenile fish and safe, shallow water.
corroboration_sources: Yorke Peninsula tourism websites (https://yorkepeninsula.com.au/explore/corny-point, https://www.visityorkepeninsula.com.au/places-to-dive-and-snorkel) - confirm Corny Point as an area with marine life and snorkelling opportunities, supporting the description of a shallow lagoon dive.

Name: Fairy Bower
Description: Gentle slope with reef ledges and swim-throughs, great visibility.
Nearby_Runoff: Some Manly urban stormwater from the peninsula; less direct runoff than adjacent Shelly Beach
Viz_Triggers: Less swell-exposed than Shelly Beach; rain events affect viz within 24-48 hours; moderate NE swell reduces viz to 4-6m
Viz_Seasonal: Summer avg: 10m, Winter avg: 7m; broadly similar to Shelly Beach but slightly more sheltered
corroboration_sources: Diveplanit.com (https://www.diveplanit.com/dive-site/fairy-bower-manly/) and PADI (https://www.padi.com/dive-site/australia/fairy-bower/) - confirms Fairy Bower as a popular shore dive in Cabbage Tree Bay Aquatic Reserve with diverse marine life.

Name: South Bondi Reef
Description: Reef and boulders at Bondi's southern end with cuttlefish and rays.
Nearby_Runoff: Southern end Bondi Beach stormwater drain; urban catchment from Bondi and Tamarama
Viz_Triggers: SE swell stirs sand rapidly; post-storm conditions worst (viz to 1-2m); strong easterly drops viz within hours
Viz_Seasonal: Autumn best (8-15m, stable); Summer variable (5-12m); Winter 5-8m; Spring poorest (plankton)
corroboration_sources: Abyss Scuba Diving (https://www.abyss.com.au/blog/dive-sites/unveil-the-charm-of-bondi-beach-a-sydney) - mentions 'Flat Rock' in South Bondi as an ideal dive site with a wide variety of marine life.

Name: Neptune Islands (Shark Cage)
Description: Famous for great white shark cage diving; pelagic zone.
corroboration_sources: Dive Worldwide (https://www.diveworldwide.com/locations/south-australia) and Marine Parks SA (https://www.marineparks.sa.gov.au/enjoy/shark-cage-diving) - confirm Neptune Islands as a unique location in Australia for great white shark cage diving.

Name: North Bondi Rocks
Description: Sheltered site with rocky outcrops and marine life near the rock pool.
Nearby_Runoff: Bondi Beach stormwater drain approximately 600m north; large urban catchment from Bondi, Bellevue Hill and surrounds
Viz_Triggers: Northerly swells churn sand; strong easterly swell drops viz to under 3m; rain events reduce viz by 50-60% for 2-3 days
Viz_Seasonal: Summer: 8-12m; Autumn best (10-15m); Winter: 6-10m; Spring: 5-8m (plankton blooms)
corroboration_sources: PADI website (https://www.padi.com/dive-site/australia/north-bondi/) - describes North Bondi as an underrated dive location with abundant marine life including sharks, rays, and "Lizzo" the blue groper. Also mentions "The Cathedral" swimthrough.

Name: Yena Gap
Description: Remote site with dramatic drop-offs and caves, accessed by long walk.
Marine_Life: Bull rays, large schools of fish
Nearby_Runoff: Botany Bay industrial and stormwater runoff from Port Botany; receives catchment from Cooks River and surrounding industrial areas
Viz_Triggers: Post-rain turbidity from Port Botany and Cooks River can last 3-5 days; north winds push murky port water towards site; best after a week of dry weather
Viz_Seasonal: Moderate 5-10m year-round; best after extended dry periods in summer; winter can be clearer in settled conditions
corroboration_sources: Michael McFadyen's Scuba Diving Web Site (https://www.michaelmcfadyenscuba.info/viewpage.php?page_id=652) - confirms remote boat dive with rocky reef, gullies, holes, caves, and marine life including Port Jackson sharks, blue devilfish, and sea dragons.

Name: Wigton Reef
Description: Pristine hard coral reef with moderate current.
corroboration_sources: General Great Barrier Reef diving information (https://greatbarrierreeftours.com/great-barrier-reef/great-barrier-reef-dive-sites/) - consistent with a liveaboard dive site in the Outer GBR with pristine reefs and pelagic sightings.

Name: Round Top Island
Description: Close-to-shore site with seagrass and some coral bommies.
corroboration_sources: YouTube video (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VKIy4386WQg) and Marine Life Network (https://marinelife.org.au/?page_id=1590) - confirm Round Top Island as a close-to-shore dive site off Mackay with fringing reefs, seagrass, and coral.

Name: Bare Island Deep Wall
Description: Wall dive adjacent to Bare Island with sponges, soft corals, and sea dragons.
Nearby_Runoff: Same as Bare Island - minor stormwater from La Perouse suburb
Viz_Triggers: Same as Bare Island - SE swell and wind reduces viz; deeper wall less affected by surface conditions
Viz_Seasonal: Same as Bare Island: Summer 8-12m, Winter 6-10m; deep wall often has better viz than the shallows
corroboration_sources: Michael McFadyen's Scuba Diving Web Site (https://www.michaelmcfadyenscuba.info/viewpage.php?page_id=262) - detailed description confirms wall dive, marine life including sea dragons, moray eels, rays, pygmy pipehorses, and shore access.

Name: Green Island – South West Rocks
Description: Small island reef with soft coral gardens.

Name: Latitude Rock
Description: Steep reef wall with bommies and good visibility.

Name: Bluefish Point
Description: Deep site with drop-offs and large schools of pelagic fish.
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal - North Head is largely national park with little development; small urban catchment from Manly area
Viz_Triggers: Exposed to NE swells; sustained NE swell over 1.5m reduces viz for 2-3 days; surge can be significant during large swells
Viz_Seasonal: Summer: 8-15m (excellent on calm days); Winter: 6-10m; best in settled anticyclonic conditions with light winds

Name: Mullaway Reef
Description: Bommie reef with good hard coral growth.

Name: Woody Head Reef
Description: Rocky reef dive near campground, ideal for macro spotting.

Name: Wedding Cake Island
Description: Rocky reef near Coogee Beach with swim-throughs and diverse marine life.
Nearby_Runoff: Coogee Beach stormwater drain approximately 300m north; urban catchment from Coogee
Viz_Triggers: Very swell-exposed; southerly storms can drop viz to 1-2m; any swell over 1m reduces viz; cold upwellings from the south can bring very clear deep water
Viz_Seasonal: Summer: 8-15m; Winter: 6-12m (upwellings can boost to 15m+); most variable site in the area - can be 2m or 20m on different days

Name: Manta Bommie
Description: A collection of rocky outcrops surrounded by a rugged rocky reef, known for manta ray encounters.
Marine_Life: Manta rays, leopard sharks, shovel nose sharks, stingrays, bull rays, octopus, crayfish, nudibranchs

Name: Bushrangers Bay
Description: Sheltered bay within an aquatic reserve known for grey nurse sharks, schools of fish, and easy access.
Marine_Life: Grey nurse sharks, rays, schools of bream

Name: Cod Hole
Description: Famous for its resident potato cod, this iconic dive site offers close encounters with massive grouper fish. The site features coral bommies and swim-throughs where these gentle giants congregate.
Marine_Life: Potato cod, Maori wrasse, reef sharks, coral trout, giant trevally
Hazards: Strong currents, deep water, large marine life requires respectful distance
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal, as it's an isolated offshore reef.
Viz_Triggers: Strong tidal currents, localized plankton blooms, and offshore weather systems.
Viz_Seasonal: Consistently excellent visibility (20-40m) year-round, but can experience seasonal thermoclines and plankton events in warmer months.

Name: Fish Rock Cave
Description: One of Australia's only true ocean swim-through caves.

Name: Magic Point
Description: Shark cave with grey nurse sharks, accessible by boat only.

Name: Rapid Bay Jetty
Description: Shore dive famous for leafy sea dragons and encrusted jetty pylons.
corroboration_sources: PADI.com, Wikivoyage, Diving Adelaide, X-Ray Mag

Name: Edithburgh Jetty
Description: Macro haven with nudibranchs, cuttlefish and leafy sea dragons under a quiet jetty.

Name: Shark Cave
Description: Cave system popular for observing grey nurse sharks.
Compass_Bearing: Not applicable for internal navigation (cave system, rely on guidelines)

Name: Wreck of the Alert
Description: Historic clipper wreck dive with structure covered in soft corals.
Compass_Bearing: Unreliable near wreck due to magnetic interference; rely on natural navigation (wreck features)
corroboration_sources: Divernet.com, The Scuba Doctor, Divers Alert Network, SCUBAPRO

Name: Stack Island
Description: Offshore island dive with caves, swim-throughs and pelagic species.
Compass_Bearing: Boat-based GPS navigation to site; underwater navigation relies on natural features and depth

Name: Rowley Shoals
Description: Remote pristine coral atolls with epic visibility and reef walls.

Nearby_Runoff: Minimal, as it's an isolated offshore reef system.
Viz_Triggers: Strong oceanic currents, localized plankton blooms, and offshore weather systems.
Viz_Seasonal: Consistently excellent visibility (20-50m) year-round, but can experience seasonal thermoclines and plankton events in warmer months.
corroboration_sources: Liveaboard.com, Dive-the-World.com, Ocean Dream Charters, Great Escape Charter Co.

Name: Eaglehawk Neck Caves
Description: Dramatic caves and kelp forests with sea dragons and sponge life.
Visibility: Good to Excellent (when conditions suitable for cave diving)
Compass_Bearing: Boat-based navigation to site; internal cave navigation relies on physical structure and diver training

Name: Julian Rocks
Description: A volcanic rock formation 2.5km offshore from Byron Bay, offering diverse marine life and excellent underwater photography opportunities. Protected marine park with year-round diving.
Marine_Life: Grey nurse sharks, leopard sharks, turtles, rays, tropical fish
Facilities: Multiple dive operators, shore dive possible
Compass_Bearing: Provided in dive briefings for site navigation
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal coastal runoff from nearby national park.
Viz_Triggers: Heavy rainfall, strong currents, and large swells.
Viz_Seasonal: Generally excellent visibility (15-30m) during calm conditions. Can be reduced after heavy rains or large swells.
corroboration_sources: Snorkeling Byron Bay, Dive in Australia

Name: HMAS Swan Wreck
Description: Decommissioned naval destroyer sunk as a dive site off Dunsborough.
Compass_Bearing: Unreliable near wreck due to magnetic interference; boat-based GPS navigation to site; rely on natural navigation (wreck features) and current awareness

Name: Osprey Reef
Description: Stunning Coral Sea atoll with wall diving, big pelagics, and pristine coral.
Marine_Life: Sharks (grey, silvertip, hammerhead), rays, reef fish
Compass_Bearing: Boat-based GPS navigation to atoll; underwater navigation relies on natural reef walls and depth
Nearby_Runoff: Minimal, as it's an isolated offshore reef.
Viz_Triggers: Strong oceanic currents, localized plankton blooms, and offshore weather systems.
Viz_Seasonal: Consistently excellent visibility (20-40m) year-round, but can experience seasonal thermoclines and plankton events in warmer months.
corroboration_sources: Mike Ball Dive Expeditions, Great Barrier Reef Liveaboards, Dive-the-World.com, Dive Planit

Name: Flinders Pier
Description: Melbournes most popular shore dive featuring a pier structure that attracts unique marine life. Excellent for night diving and macro photography.
Marine_Life: Weedy sea dragons, seahorses, octopus, pipefish, crabs
Hazards: Cold water, limited visibility, pier structure
Compass_Bearing: Navigation relies on pier structure; magnetic interference expected under metal structure; shore entry
Nearby_Runoff: Moderate runoff from local town and pier activities.
Viz_Triggers: Heavy rainfall, strong tidal currents, and disturbance from human activity.
Viz_Seasonal: Variable visibility (2-8m), often better on incoming tides during calm conditions. Can be very murky after rain or with strong currents.

Name: Portsea Pier
Description: Pier dive with prolific macro life including seahorses and nudibranchs.
Visibility: Variable, often better on incoming and high tides (sufficient light to see the bottom)
Compass_Bearing: Navigation relies on pier structure; magnetic interference expected under metal structure

Name: SS Annie M. Miller
Description: Historic trawler wreck encrusted with marine life and upright on the seabed.
Visibility: Variable (typically good, but can be very good or poor depending on conditions and proximity to Sydney Harbour mouth)
Compass_Bearing: GPS to site (33° 52.0051' S 151° 17.9353' E); internal wreck navigation relies on visual cues (rough N/S orientation) due to magnetic interference; surface bearings and marks advised
Nearby_Runoff: Sydney Harbour catchment; multiple stormwater inflows across the harbour after heavy rain
Viz_Triggers: Heavy rain drops viz to 2-3m for 2-3 days; port traffic and vessel turbulence reduces viz in shallows; best after 5+ dry days
Viz_Seasonal: Consistent 5-10m year-round; best after extended dry spells; summer slightly better (8-10m) than winter (5-8m)
corroboration_sources: Michael McFadyen's Scuba Diving Web Site

Name: Five Fathom Reef
Description: Popular reef system near Perth with hard corals, reef sharks, and rays.
Visibility: Good, but can be impacted by swell (best in low swell under 1m)
Compass_Bearing: GPS to site; underwater navigation relies on natural reef features and wreck structure; compasses may be unreliable near wreck
corroboration_sources: Diving WA Wrecks, Rockingham Visitor Centre Snorkelling and Diving Guide

Name: Clarkes Reef
Description: Scenic fringing reef off Albany with bommies and swim-throughs.
Visibility: Variable (generally better in winter months, typical for Albany region)
Compass_Bearing: Boat-based GPS navigation to site; underwater navigation relies on natural reef formations
corroboration_sources: Divernet.com (regional conditions), general reef diving knowledge

Name: Kilsby Sinkhole
Description: Crystal-clear freshwater sinkhole with open-top access. Great light rays.
Compass_Bearing: Internal navigation relies on visual cues within the sinkhole; no external compass bearing for site travel
corroboration_sources: Kilsbysinkhole.com, Southaustralia.com, Oceanographic Magazine, Eunoia Freediving

Name: Piccaninnie Ponds
Description: UNESCO-listed wetland with cathedral-like chasms and haloclines.

Name: Tank Cave

Nearby_Runoff: Minimal due to remote, offshore location.
Viz_Triggers: Strong oceanic currents, localized plankton blooms, and offshore weather systems.

Nearby_Runoff: Nil, entirely underground system.
Viz_Triggers: Silt disturbance by divers, unusual geological activity.
